questions.text: Percussion is defined as:

answers.text: Listening for sounds with a stethoscope
The act of tapping on a surface to evaluate the underlying structures
Using the hands to feel for lumps and masses

questions.text: During chest percussion, the stationary finger should be placed:

answers.text: Perpendicular to the ribs with the palm flat on the chest
Parallel to the ribs, with the palm and other fingers held off the chest

questions.text: Which finger of the non-dominant hand is placed against the chest during percussion?
The striking motion during chest percussion should be generated at the:
For the clearest percussion note, the two fingers should be in contact for:

answers.text: The length of one full breath

questions.text: A resonant percussion note is associated with:
A flat percussion note is described as soft, high-pitched, and of short duration — it is associated with:
A dull percussion note is characterized by:

answers.text: Very loud, low pitch, long duration

questions.text: A dull percussion note is heard over all of the following EXCEPT:
A hyperresonant percussion note suggests:
A tympanic percussion note is best described as:

answers.text: Loud and drum-like, with a high pitch

questions.text: A tympanic sound is most commonly heard over a:
Which percussion sound is described as very loud, lower in pitch, and longer in duration than a resonant note?
Auscultation is defined as:

answers.text: The process of listening to sounds in the body
Tapping on a surface to evaluate underlying structures

questions.text: Which is the most commonly used physical assessment technique of the respiratory and cardiovascular system?
When performing lung auscultation, the patient should be:

answers.text: Lying supine with the head flat
Sitting upright, breathing a little deeper through an open mouth
Lying on the left lateral side

questions.text: Why should stethoscope tubing not touch anything during auscultation?
Which is NOT part of analyzing breath sounds?

answers.text: Identifying when during the respiratory cycle it is heard
Determining if the sound changes with coughing

questions.text: Vesicular breath sounds are heard over:
Bronchovesicular breath sounds are heard over:
Bronchial or tracheal breath sounds are heard:

answers.text: Over most of the lung fields

questions.text: Adventitious breath sounds are best defined as:

answers.text: Normal airflow sounds over healthy lung
Added, irregular sounds produced within a normal sound
Sounds produced by the heart valves
Sounds produced by vocal cord vibration

questions.text: When auscultating coarse rales, the most appropriate recommendation is:
When auscultating medium rales, the most appropriate recommendation is:
When fine rales are auscultated, the recommended interventions include:

answers.text: IPPB, heart drugs, diuretics, and oxygen

questions.text: Unilateral (localized) wheezing most likely suggests:
Cardiac asthma is defined as:

answers.text: Wheezing and dyspnea in patients with heart failure (CHF) in the absence of asthma or COPD
A reactive airway disease of childhood

questions.text: Cardiac asthma is caused by:

answers.text: Vascular congestion and perivascular edema from fluid overload

questions.text: Why do voice sounds through a normal air-filled lung sound muffled and unclear?

answers.text: Because the diaphragm dampens the sound
Because sound vibrations travel poorly through air
Because the alveoli absorb all sound
Because of reflection from the ribs

questions.text: Vocal resonance is increased when sound travels through a:
Egophony is defined as:

answers.text: A low-pitched rumbling heard in pneumothorax
Abnormally enhanced vocal resonance with a high-pitched, nasal quality
Soft, clear voice heard over normal lung

questions.text: To test for egophony, the patient repeats:
Egophony exists when the patient's 'E' is heard through the stethoscope as:
Egophony indicates:
Bronchophony is best defined as:

answers.text: Muffled voice transmission through air-filled lung
Abnormally loud and clear transmission of voice sounds through an area of increased density
Silent voice transmission over a pneumothorax
A whispered voice heard faintly through the chest wall

questions.text: To test for bronchophony, the patient repeats:
Whispered pectoriloquy is compared to bronchophony as being:
To assess for whispered pectoriloquy, the patient is asked to whisper:
If whispered words are heard loudly and distinctly through the chest wall, you should suspect:
Normal heart sounds are caused by:
The aortic region is auscultated at:

answers.text: The 2nd – 3rd intercostal spaces at the right sternal border
The 2nd – 3rd intercostal spaces at the left sternal border
The apex in the mid-clavicular line
The 5th intercostal space, right sternal border

questions.text: The pulmonic region is auscultated at:

answers.text: The 2nd – 3rd intercostal spaces at the right sternal border
The 2nd – 3rd intercostal spaces at the left sternal border
The 3rd – 6th intercostal spaces at the left sternal border
The mid-clavicular line at the apex

questions.text: The tricuspid region is auscultated at:

answers.text: The 2nd – 3rd intercostal spaces at the right sternal border
The 3rd, 4th, 5th, and 6th intercostal spaces at the left sternal border
Near the apex in the mid-clavicular line

questions.text: The mitral region is auscultated near the apex of the heart at:

answers.text: The 2nd – 3rd intercostal spaces, right sternal border
The 5th – 6th intercostal spaces in the mid-clavicular line
The 3rd – 6th intercostal spaces, left sternal border

questions.text: S1 corresponds to:

answers.text: Closure of the aortic and pulmonic valves
Closure of the atrioventricular (mitral and tricuspid) valves

questions.text: S1 occurs at:
S2 corresponds to:

answers.text: Closure of the mitral and tricuspid valves
Closure of the aortic and pulmonic valves

questions.text: S2 occurs when:
S3 and S4 are both best heard at:

answers.text: The right sternal border, 2nd intercostal space
The left 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line

questions.text: S3 results from:

answers.text: Atrial contraction against a closed valve

questions.text: An S3 heart sound may suggest:
S4 is most often caused by:

answers.text: A stiff ventricle, as in hypertension or after a myocardial infarction

questions.text: Murmurs are best described as:

answers.text: Extra sounds heard in conjunction with S1 and S2

questions.text: Murmurs are caused by:

answers.text: Blood regurgitating into the chamber it came from, or blood pushed through an abnormal opening

questions.text: Which of the following abnormal openings is associated with murmurs?

answers.text: Atrial septal defect (ASD) or patent ductus arteriosus (PDA)

questions.text: A primary indication for pulse oximetry is:

answers.text: Monitoring adequacy of arterial oxyhemoglobin saturation

questions.text: Pulse oximetry can be used to:

answers.text: Replace an ABG in all clinical scenarios
Quantify the patient's response to therapeutic interventions and diagnostic procedures

questions.text: Regarding pulse oximetry, which statement is correct?

answers.text: No significant contraindications are noted; an ABG may be more properly warranted for additional information
It is contraindicated in all ICU patients
It cannot be used on the ear

questions.text: Precautions when using pulse oximetry include:
SpO₂ and SaO₂ differ in that:

answers.text: They are the same thing with two names
SpO₂ refers to pulse oximetry; SaO₂ is measured with hemoximetry
SaO₂ is measured by the oximeter only
SpO₂ is measured invasively and SaO₂ noninvasively

questions.text: Pulse oximetry measures blood Hgb saturations using:
Pulse oximetry operates using the:
The Lambert-Beer law describes:

answers.text: How blood pressure changes with vessel diameter
Light absorbed and transmitted by a substance to identify its presence and determine its concentration
How pressure is distributed in a closed system

questions.text: Pulse oximetry uses two wavelengths of light — specifically:
Pulse oximetry measures transmission through:

answers.text: Living tissue such as a finger or earlobe

questions.text: In a pulse oximetry sensor, the photodetector:

answers.text: Emits the red and infrared beams
Measures the light on the opposite side from the emitter

questions.text: In a pulse oximetry signal, the BASELINE represents:

answers.text: Pulse component caused by intermittent arterial flow
Stable absorbance of the tissue bed

questions.text: In a pulse oximetry signal, the PEAK represents:

answers.text: Stable absorbance of the tissue bed
The pulse component caused by intermittent arterial flow through the tissues

questions.text: When performing pulse oximetry, you should:

answers.text: Apply firm pressure to occlude the artery
Place the sensor on the selected site and observe the waveform if available
Warm the site to 40°C before placement
Keep the probe in place for no more than 10 seconds

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT specifically listed as documentation required for a pulse oximetry measurement?
Which inspired gas parameter must be documented when recording pulse oximetry?

answers.text: FiO₂ being provided to the patient

questions.text: Which combination correctly pairs a lung percussion sound with its cause?
A pneumothorax would be expected to produce which percussion note?
Pleural effusion would be expected to produce which percussion note?
Which voice auscultation test is the MOST SENSITIVE for detecting consolidation?
Which pair correctly matches the valve set with its heart sound?
